Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative declarative proverb; noun + elative source phrase + allative adverbial + negative auxiliary + present verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Children or close followers often resemble their parents, family, or social model in behavior and character.
Usage Contexts: family, village gossip, upbringing, humor, moral comparison
Register and Tone: colloquial proverb; gently ironic or affectionate
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Finnic family-resemblance proverb known in Votic proverb tradition; especially natural in kinship-centered Ingria village contexts.
First Example Sentence: Kui Martti pajatõb niku izä, ämme nagrõb: õuna õunassa kaukallõ eb lankõ.
Second Example Sentence: Tüttö tehäb tüüte vaiksõssi niku emä, i kõik juttõvad, etti õuna õunassa kaukallõ eb lankõ.
Third Example Sentence: Kuz meill noorõd õppõvad vanoilta, siiz nätäb: õuna õunassa kaukallõ eb lankõ.

Idiom (Votic): Iga ühs on oma õnnõ päällä seppä.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Equative nominal clause; universal pronoun subject + copula + possessive genitive phrase + adessive + predicate noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person is responsible for shaping their own future through choices and effort.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Inimin teeb ize õma eloo teed.
Usage Contexts: advice, work ethic, education, migration decisions, personal responsibility
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Craft metaphor built around the smith as maker; suits rural and artisan imagery in Finnic proverb culture.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Votic): /ˈiɡɑ yhs on ˈomɑ ˈɤnːɤ ˈpæːlːæ ˈsepːæ/
First Example Sentence: Älä ootõ võõrassa apua ain: iga ühs on oma õnnõ päällä seppä.
Second Example Sentence: Kui siä tahod õppia vad'd'a tšeeltä, muista, etti iga ühs on oma õnnõ päällä seppä.
Third Example Sentence: Vana dääde juttõli pojallõ: tee tüüte süämeeka, iga ühs on oma õnnõ päällä seppä.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical imperative proverb; numeral object phrase + imperative verb + temporal connector + imperative verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Think carefully and check facts before taking an irreversible action.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Enne tüüte piäb kõik üvässi kattsoa.
Usage Contexts: craft, planning, teaching, workplace, warnings against haste
Register and Tone: practical proverb; instructional, calm, mildly admonishing
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Carpentry and household-work wisdom; tied to measurement, repair, and careful manual labor.
First Example Sentence: Älä kirjuta nime lepingussõ enne kui loõd: kõlmõd kõrtaa mittaa, siz leikkaa.
Second Example Sentence: Kui võrkkoa paikkaammõ, vana kalastaja juttõb: kõlmõd kõrtaa mittaa, siz leikkaa.
Third Example Sentence: Škouluz õpõttõjõ ütlõb lahzillõ, etti kõlmõd kõrtaa mittaa, siz leikkaa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical comparative nominal proverb; demonstrative-correlative pattern with coordinated kinship nouns.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A daughter often resembles her mother in habits, voice, skills, or temperament.
Usage Contexts: family, domestic life, humor, praise, mild teasing
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kinship-based proverb reflecting close observation of family roles and inherited habits in small communities.
First Example Sentence: Kun tütär laulõb pulmõz niku emä, kõik sanõvad: millen emä, mokom i tütär.
Second Example Sentence: Ämmä näki, kui tütär praavitti rooppaa, i nagrõzi: millen emä, mokom i tütär.
Third Example Sentence: Liivčüläz jutõlti pehmiässi: millen emä, mokom i tütär.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical comparative nominal proverb; correlative adjective with father/son kinship pairing.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A son often copies or inherits the father’s behavior, skills, faults, or public reputation.
Usage Contexts: family, work habits, village reputation, humor, gentle criticism
Register and Tone: colloquial proverb; playful, approving, or mildly critical
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Companion kinship proverb to mother-daughter comparison; reflects intergenerational apprenticeship and household continuity.
First Example Sentence: Pokatsi parantõb venee niku izä, no millin isä, mokomain pokatsi.
Second Example Sentence: Kui poika ain hiljõssi tuõb tüüss, naapuri ütli: millin isä, mokomain pokatsi.
Third Example Sentence: Dääde kuuntõli poikaa i sanõzi rõõmukkaassi: millin isä, mokomain pokatsi.

Idiom (Votic): Čen eb tee, se eb süü.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-correlative negative clause; animate relative pronoun + negated verb + resumptive pronoun + negated verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Benefits should come with effort; a person who refuses to contribute should not expect reward.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ilma tüüttä inimin eb saa palkaa ni süümissä.
Usage Contexts: workplace, household chores, farming, moral lessons, community duties
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Work-and-food proverb common in agrarian communities; ties survival to shared labor.
First Example Sentence: Kui noorõd istuvad va, emä muistutab: čen eb tee, se eb süü.
Second Example Sentence: Talguuz kõik auttavad, sillä čen eb tee, se eb süü.
Third Example Sentence: Dääde eb vihastu, a ütli kõvassi: čen eb tee, se eb süü.

Idiom (Votic): Ühess kõrvõss kuulõn, tõizõss lazzõn vällää.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed first-person idiomatic clause; elative source phrase + present verb + second elative phrase + causative/release verb + directional adverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To ignore advice immediately after hearing it; to let information pass through without attention.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Miä kuulõn, a en piä meelez.
Usage Contexts: family scolding, classroom, joking self-criticism, warnings, informal conversation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Body-part idiom shared broadly across European proverb traditions and attested in Votic wording.
First Example Sentence: Õpõttõjõ pajatti pitkää, a poika ühess kõrvõss kuulõn, tõizõss lazzõn vällää.
Second Example Sentence: Älä tee niku enne: ühess kõrvõss kuulõn, tõizõss lazzõn vällää.
Third Example Sentence: Miä nagran, a tõttõ se on: ühess kõrvõss kuulõn, tõizõss lazzõn vällää.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal subject proverb; adjective + noun subject + frequency adverb + comparative adverb + present verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A new person, tool, or system often performs energetically at first, sometimes better than old routines.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Uusi tüütečijä algõz teeb tüüte kõvassi üvässi.
Usage Contexts: workplace, politics, household change, tools, ironic evaluation of newcomers
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Household-cleaning metaphor from broom use; applied to new workers, officials, habits, or tools.
First Example Sentence: Uusi starosta lupazi kõikka parantaa; rahvaz ütli: uusi mätla ain parepassi pühib.
Second Example Sentence: Kui ostoimma uuvvõ masina, dääde nagrõzi: uusi mätla ain parepassi pühib.
Third Example Sentence: Tüüpaikkõz uus inženerõ tuli varhain, i kõik muistõtti: uusi mätla ain parepassi pühib.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Resultative clause; universal pronoun subject + inchoative verb + reduplicative expressive predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Everything becomes messy, confused, disordered, or emotionally chaotic.
Usage Contexts: home, meetings, bureaucracy, children’s play, emotional stress, comic complaint
Register and Tone: colloquial expressive idiom; humorous, frustrated, vivid
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reduplicative sound-symbolic expression; such echo-forms are common in Finnic expressive vocabulary.
First Example Sentence: Kui lahzõd johsõvad rihez, kõik jääb mürri-marri.
Second Example Sentence: Ilma nimekirjatta koosõolõkuz kõik jääb mürri-marri.
Third Example Sentence: Älä pane kaikkia laukkuu ühez: kõik jääb mürri-marri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Concise existential/location clause; abstract noun subject + copula + reflexive locative complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Happiness or luck is found within oneself rather than only in outside places or possessions.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Inimin võib õlla õnnõkõz õmass süämess.
Usage Contexts: reflection, advice, migration, family consolation, literature, spiritual speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Introspective village saying linked to local ideas of home, restraint, and inner steadiness; supported by Votic sentence tradition.
First Example Sentence: Čülä kujallõ meed ettsimä õnnõa, a õnni on enez.
Second Example Sentence: Kui süämez on rauha, siiz tääd, etti õnni on enez.
Third Example Sentence: Vana naizikko juttõli lapsõlõ: ära itke, õnni on enez.
